claims.text: Nostradamus is widely credited with having predicted nearly every significant global event of the past 500 years — Hitler, the atomic bombs, JFK's assassination, 9/11, Princess Diana's death, the COVID-19 pandemic — with proponents claiming roughly 70% accuracy.

evidence.quote:
Well, some websites say he's been 70 percent accurate.
That's pretty good. So let's look at some of his more famous predictions that came true
and learn more about the man

claims.rationale: It is true that popular media credits Nostradamus with predicting all these events. The 70% accuracy figure is a meme that circulates online without methodological grounding. Whether the predictions actually correspond to events vs. being post-hoc reverse-engineered is the central question the rest of the video answers (largely in the 'reverse-engineered' direction).

claims.text: Nostradamus's most famous predicted-while-still-alive case — the 1559 jousting death of King Henry II of France — closely matches the quatrain 'the young lion will overcome the older one... his eyes through a golden cage... two wounds... cruel death,' since young Comte de Montgomery shattered his lance through Henry's gilded visor causing two wounds and Henry died of sepsis 11 days later.

evidence.quote:
Nostradamus may have accurately predicted the untimely demise of Henry, the second king of
France, who was only 40 when he died, jousting in fifteen fifty nine.
The young lion we overcome the older one on the field of combat.
In a single battle, he would have his eyes through a golden cage to wounds made one.
Then he dies a cruel death.
Let's break down the prediction.
The young lion will overcome the older one.
As Henry was jousting Gabrielle Delors, one of his young soldiers when he was killed,
they both had lions on their shields and Henry actually wore armor shaped like a lion.
How do we know? Well, Henry's armor still around Pierce's eyes for a golden cage.
So during the joust, the young lions lance shattered, driving splinters through the
king's visor and into his eyes and skull, creating two wounds.
And yes, the armor was gold.
As for the cruel death, Henry was still conscious after his injury.
He even got up and walked.
Though he was a mess, the king experienced seizures, partial paralysis, and despite
having the best medical care available at the time, he died in agony of sepsis.
Eleven days later.
So creepy. Yeah, uncanny.
I think so. And with that prediction, the legend of Nostradamus was born

claims.rationale: Henry II of France did die from a jousting wound from Gabriel de Lorges, comte de Montgomery on July 10, 1559 (he died July 10, 11 days after the wound on June 30). The 'young lion / older lion / golden cage / two wounds / cruel death' quatrain (Century I, quatrain 35) does exist in Nostradamus's Prophecies. However: (a) the host's later debunking concedes the 'young lion' lines themselves were added years later; (b) Nostradamus did not name Henry, the date, or the manner explicitly enough to constitute a verified prediction in advance — the connection was made retrospectively. Real event, real quatrain, debatable predictive force.

claims.text: The 'rise of Hitler' Nostradamus quatrain reads remarkably close to Hitler's biography (born poor in the West, in his younger days unfit but a great speech-giver, conquered Europe, defeated by 'Hister' which differs from Hitler by one letter) — but 'Hister' is actually the Latin name for the Danube River, not a person, and Erika Cheetham re-translated the same passage to point at Gaddafi, then Khomeini, then Saddam Hussein as politically convenient.

evidence.quote:
Many believe Nostradamus predicted the rise of Hitler from the depths of the west of
Europe, a young child will be born of poor people.
Hitler was born in Austria and came from a lower to middle class family, except he who by
his tongue will seduce a great tooth.
His film will increase towards the end of the east and his younger days, his military
career was undistinguished.
He didn't have money or influence, but he did know how to whip up a crowd with a speech
kicks out beasts ferocious with hunger across the Vivace
conquered Europe and just a few years and always wanted more.
The greatest part of the battlefield will be against his death.
And that's just one letter off

The most creative of Nostradamus interpreters and the one that I first read was also an
ardent believer.
That was Erika Cheatham.
She's best known for reinterpreting Nostradamus reference to history as a reference to
Hitler.
But his daughter was actually a part of the Danube River that Nostradamus knew quite
well, and Cheatham adjusted her translations to suit the times.
Occasionally, she made future predictions, and when they didn't happen, she'd issue
updates of her books with new names and new dates.
First, Nostradamus was talking about Moammar Gadhafi and later changed that to Ayatollah
Khomeini and then later changed it to Saddam Hussein

claims.rationale: The Hitler/Hister quatrain (II.24, 'Bestes farouches de faim fluves tranner / Plus part du champ encore Hister sera') is real and Hister is indeed the Latin name for the lower Danube River, not a person — this is well-established in Nostradamus scholarship. Erika Cheetham (real translator, The Prophecies of Nostradamus, 1973+) did revise her translations to fit successive contemporary villains as the host says. The host correctly cites the famous quatrain and correctly notes the Hister=river debunking and Cheetham's serial reinterpretation — this is a fair summary of how the Hitler 'prediction' actually works.

claims.text: The famous Nostradamus 9/11 prediction circulating online ('two steel birds will fall from the sky on the metropolis... 45 degrees... fire approaches the great new city... immediately a huge scattered flame leaps up') is a fabrication: those lines were added to internet folklore after September 11, 2001 and were never written by Nostradamus.

evidence.quote:
Did Nostradamus see the 9/11 attacks to steal birds fall from the sky on the propolis, the
steel birds have to be planes and the
metropolis, New York jets out of the sky, the next forty five degrees.
New York is 40 degrees latitude.
But that's that's pretty close.
Fire approaches the great new CD immediately.
A huge scattered flame leaps up.
New York was a new city when Nostradamus was alive and the fire in flames leaping up.
Yeah, that happened.
We see moths.
The undead will roll on the air.
That sounds like zombies.
Now, obviously, there were no zombies after 9/11, but I'm from New York every day as a
New Yorker, we expected another attack.
New Yorkers weren't actual zombies after 9/11, but we acted like them
Those lines were added after the 9/11 attacks and just became part of the folklore

claims.rationale: The 'steel birds / 45 degrees / new city' 9/11 quatrain is a confirmed hoax: it was circulated on email chains starting September 12, 2001, was traced to a 1997 essay by Brock University student Neil Marshall as a deliberate parody to demonstrate how easy it is to fake Nostradamus quatrains, then got pasted into actual circulation as if real. Snopes and major fact-checkers have debunked it. The host gets this exactly right.

claims.text: Most popular biographical claims about Nostradamus are myth: he was not a converted Jew (his grandfather was), he never studied or taught medicine at Montpellier, he didn't deduce heliocentrism before Copernicus, he wasn't on the Vatican's Index of Forbidden Books, he never knelt before a future pope, he didn't use a 'magic mirror', he wasn't buried upright with a death-date medallion, and his prophecies were almost unknown in his lifetime.

evidence.quote:
Now that we've covered the myths, let's look at the facts now, Michelle Nostradamus
did lead an extraordinary life,
but he was not a Jew who converted to Christianity.
One of his grandfathers did, but he didn't.
He inherited nothing special from the Isaac tribe.
He had no grandfathers who were doctors in the royal court or anywhere.
He didn't study medicine in Montpelier.
He never went there.
Definitely didn't teach there.
Actually, during that time, he wandered the countryside from fifteen, twenty one to
fifteen, twenty nine and taught himself apothecary.
His wife and two children did die from disease, but probably wasn't the plague.
He didn't use antiseptics.
Nobody did. And he didn't recommend a low fat diet or exercise, though that's still a
good idea.
He made no discoveries about the solar system and his only alleged contact with the
Inquisition was an invitation to comment on metallurgy and bronze casting.
Being on the Vatican's index of forbidden books sounds pretty bad ass, but he wasn't on
it.
He was actually on good terms with the church, which is probably why he was invited to be
a guest speaker at Inquisition or whatever that was that kneeling before the young pope
story. Pure fiction never happened.
As far as his fame.
Nostradamus prophecies were virtually unknown during his lifetime, but he did gain some
notoriety from writing cookbooks.
I'm sorry, did you think cookbook skip cookbooks, you know, recipes for how to cook a
nice piece of fish food.
You also wrote almanacs that sold pretty well, but they weren't very accurate.
His prophecies were not called the centuries.
They were called the prophecies.
They were not written in code.
They were in rhyming verse.
Oh, like that is trying to get us canceled.
Nostradamus did not use a bowl of water is his magic mirror.
When he was writing, his prophecies were composing his poems.
He used a regular mirror.
I'll leave it to you to determine whether it's magic or not.
He missed the year of his death by one, although some versions of his writing show the
date was later changed, which probably doesn't count.
He was not buried upright and there's no record of any medallion or anything else with a
date written anywhere.
But the only well known fact that's true about Nostradamus is that he was a fairly
reputable plague doctor

claims.rationale: These debunkings are largely correct per Stéphane Gerson's biography 'Nostradamus: How an Obscure Renaissance Astrologer Became the Modern Prophet of Doom' (2012) and other serious historical work. His grandfather Pierre de Nostredame did convert from Judaism. He learned apothecary informally before Montpellier admitted him briefly (he was expelled). He had no peer-reviewed astronomical contributions. He was never on the Index. The kneeling-before-future-pope and buried-upright-with-medallion stories are 19th-century legends. The host gets the historiography right.

claims.text: Nostradamus did write his book The Prophecies in 1555, comprising over a thousand quatrains in middle-French rhyming verse — but the writing is symbolic and allegorical enough that translators have to take poetic license, which lets interpreters reverse-engineer any past event into apparent prophecy.

evidence.quote:
Nostradamus remains famous over four hundred years after his death
for a fifteen fifty five book he wrote titled The Prophecies.
The book is a collection of over a thousand quatrains, which are for line rhyming verses
which are said to foretell the future.
Now, depending on who you believe, Nostradamus has been credited with accurately
predicting Hiroshima, Hitler, the space shuttle disaster, the Apollo moon landing, both
world wars, the current pandemic on and on, everything that ever happened, he called it.
In fact, good luck finding some significant global event that Nostradamus was not said to
have predicted.
The truth is, nobody has ever used Nostradamus writings to predict a future event.
Never one time that ever once, never.
So why do so many people still believe for the same reason I wrote this episode, for the
same reason you clicked on it?
It's fascinating and fun.
Nostradamus is a great story and we never let the facts get in the way of a great story.
So what went wrong?
Well, the problem with Nostradamus is predictions is how his writing is translated and
then interpreted.
He wrote in middle French and you really can't do a word for word translation into
English or even modern French.
When his writing is translated, there's a little poetic license required to fill in the
gaps.
And that's OK. There are certain translations of Dante's Divine Comedy, which was written
in vulgar Italian that are better than other translations.
There is a certain creativity to it, but nobody's building bunkers based on DONTAE.
And if you are awesome and Nostradamus was not really specific about anything, his verses
are all symbolism and allegory.
You can make any story fit if you want to be.
Nostradamus talks about a great lady.
Does he mean a literal lady or does he mean a country or a city?
Well, depends on what you want the end result of the prophecy to be.
It's kind of like reverse engineering the prophecy.
You know what event took place.
Now you just go back and make the prophecy fit.
If you have to be a little creative, so be it

claims.rationale: The Prophecies (Les Prophéties) was first published 1555 with subsequent expanded editions, and the standard arrangement is 942 surviving quatrains organized in 'centuries' of 100 each (the host's 'over a thousand' is in the right ballpark, with some incomplete centuries). The middle-French + symbolic-allegorical-style nature of the verses, and the resulting interpretive elasticity, is well established in Nostradamus scholarship. The host's framing is accurate.

claims.text: No one has ever successfully used Nostradamus's writings to predict a future event in advance — every attributed 'prediction' has been retrofitted after the event happened.

evidence.quote:
The truth is, nobody has ever used Nostradamus writings to predict a future event.
Never one time that ever once, never

claims.rationale: This is the academic consensus and the published view of historians like James Randi (The Mask of Nostradamus, 1990) and Stéphane Gerson (2012). No documented case of an in-advance Nostradamus-derived prediction matching a future event exists; all famous matches are retrospective. This is the strongest claim in the video and is well supported.

dependencies.rationale: Directly contradicts the framing that Nostradamus is ~70% accurate.
Implies even the famous Henry II 'prediction' was retrofitted (consistent with the added-later young-lion lines).

claims.text: Belief in apocalyptic prophecy is a recurring human pattern — the millennial Y2K panic, the 2012 Mayan calendar scare, every-era cycle — driven by the psychological need to find order in chaos and to believe disasters were inevitable parts of a larger plan.

evidence.quote:
In one thousand A.D., everyone thought the world is going to end in two
thousand.
Same thing. We called it Y2K, but it was the same psychology.
I remember the great Mayan calendar scare of twenty twelve same psychology.
People look for answers and prophecies because they suggest the disaster couldn't have
been avoided.
It is part of a larger plan and there's comfort in that.
The human mind is wired to create order from chaos to see patterns and connections, even
when none really exist, to be in control of our minds and our environment

claims.rationale: The Y2K panic, the 2012 Mayan-calendar scare, the year-1000 millennial expectations (more contested historically — recent medievalist work by Richard Landes shows the 'Terrors of the Year 1000' was itself somewhat constructed but the underlying pattern is real), and apophenia / pattern-finding under uncertainty are all well-documented. The host's psychology framing aligns with mainstream cognitive-bias research (Daniel Kahneman, etc.).
